mapping_patterns.name: One source to one planning unit
mapping_patterns.description: Use when one source element is already atomic and implementation-sized.

mapping_patterns.name: One source to many planning units
mapping_patterns.description: Use when one source element contains several independently implementable obligations.

mapping_patterns.name: Many sources to one planning unit
mapping_patterns.description: Use when several source elements together describe one inseparable obligation.

blocking_conditions.description: The source element has no stable identifier.
The source fragment cannot be restated as a concrete obligation.
An ambiguity affecting the source material is marked as blocking for downstream work.
The source element readiness is explicitly blocked.

partial_conditions.description: Primary source material is usable, but a supporting view is incomplete.
Core behavior is clear, but constraints are underspecified.
Some desired cross-view trace links are missing even though decomposition can still proceed.

split_when: The source obligation crosses different technical boundaries.
The work cannot be completed credibly in one focused pull request.
Different parts of the obligation can be verified independently.
The obligation mixes behavior and infrastructure concerns that should be reviewed separately.
keep_together_when: The same acceptance proof applies to the whole obligation.
The same invariant or guard governs the full behavior.
Separate implementation would create artificial sequencing or trace drift.
merge_when: Multiple spec units are inseparable for implementation.
Several source elements describe one tightly coupled behavior.
Separate issues would create noise without adding verification value.

spec_unit_strategy: Split composite requirements into separate spec units when they contain multiple independent obligations.
Keep cross-cutting non-functional constraints as standalone spec units unless inseparable from one specific behavior.
Do not derive implementation slices from vague or aspirational language.
Derive a candidate spec unit from each main-flow step when the step expresses a concrete system obligation.
Derive separate candidate spec units for alternate and exception flows when they create independently meaningful behavior.
Treat preconditions and postconditions as constraints unless they themselves define an implementation slice.
Split by actor-to-system responsibility boundary when a step mixes user action and system action.
Derive spec units from scenario steps only when the scenario adds operational specificity beyond the use case.
Do not duplicate a use case step and a scenario step into separate units when the scenario restates the same obligation.
Use scenarios to enrich or refine, not to create parallel drift.
Derive spec units from entity lifecycle obligations.
Derive separate spec units for domain invariants when they constrain behavior independently.
Derive relationship-maintenance obligations only when they imply implementation behavior.
Derive spec units from observable message obligations.
Group tightly coupled request-response exchanges into one spec unit when splitting would destroy the interaction contract.
Split at integration boundary crossings when separate implementation slices are likely.
Derive spec units from transitions that represent distinct business behavior.
Derive separate spec units for guard conditions when they impose independent acceptance constraints.
Treat invalid and terminal transitions as explicit obligations when they affect observable behavior.
Derive standalone spec units for constraints that apply across many behaviors.
Embed a constraint into an implementation unit only when it is local to that unit.
Preserve source linkage to the originating invariant, precondition, postcondition, or non-functional constraint.
